title: Prolonged Fever with Abdominal Discomfort
subtitle: Medicine Ward · Day 3 of Admission

clinicalPresentation.chiefComplaint: Stepwise rising fever for 10 days with headache, abdominal discomfort and loose stools

HOPI.onset: Insidious onset low-grade fever 10 days ago, gradually rising in a stepwise pattern each day
HOPI.progression: Fever now reaching 39.5°C with predominantly evening spikes. Frontal headache and generalised malaise throughout. Loose stools (3–4/day, no blood) and abdominal distension appeared on day 5. Epistaxis on day 8.
associatedSymptoms: Loose stools 3–4/day — no blood, no mucus
One episode of epistaxis on day 8

familyHistory: No similar illness in hostel mates
personalHistory: Eats from hostel canteen and roadside stalls regularly
Drinks tap water at hostel — no filtration

abdominal: Mild hepatomegaly — liver 2 cm below costal margin
others: Rose spots: 2–3 faint pink maculopapular lesions on upper abdomen — easily missed
CVS: Pulse 78 bpm despite temperature 39.2°C — relative bradycardia

clinicalClue: KEY FINDING: Relative bradycardia — pulse inappropriately low for the degree of fever. Most febrile illnesses cause tachycardia proportional to temperature rise. Combined with faint rose spots on the abdomen, stepwise fever, and leucopenia, this points strongly away from common viral infections.

symptoms: More rose spots appearing on trunk
effects.penaltyMsg: Untreated infection progressing. Intestinal involvement increasing with complication risk rising.
Intestinal complication has occurred. Emergency surgical management is now required alongside antibiotics.
Patient died from complications of untreated enteric fever. Timely antibiotics and clinical monitoring were the key interventions.

results.early_febrile: WBC: 3,800/μL (leucopenia). Lymphocyte predominance. Hb 10.8 g/dL. Platelets: 140,000/μL (mildly low).
results.haemodynamic_compromise: WBC: 18,000/μL (new leucocytosis — significant change). Hb 7.2 g/dL. Platelets: 62,000/μL. Band forms present.

interpretation.early_febrile: Leucopenia in a febrile patient is opposite to most bacterial infections. Classic for enteric fever and some viral causes. Narrows DDx significantly.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Persistent leucopenia with worsening anaemia and thrombocytopenia — continuing systemic course.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: New leucocytosis in a previously leucopenic patient signals intestinal perforation or secondary infection. Surgical emergency.

tests.name: MP Smear
tests.fullName: Malaria Parasite Thick and Thin Smear

results.early_febrile: No malaria parasites seen. Giemsa stain negative for P. falciparum and P. vivax.
results.intestinal_phase: Repeat smear: NEGATIVE for malaria parasites.

interpretation.early_febrile: Malaria ruled out — essential differential in febrile patient from Kathmandu. Enteric fever remains top differential.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Malaria excluded. Complication is not from malaria.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Malaria not contributing to haemodynamic deterioration.

results.early_febrile: Malaria RDT: NEGATIVE — P. falciparum and P. vivax antigens absent.

interpretation.early_febrile: Rapid malaria exclusion. Higher sensitivity than smear for low parasitaemia P. falciparum. Malaria DDx safely eliminated.

tests.name: Widal Test
tests.fullName: Widal Test — O and H Agglutination Titres

results.early_febrile: TO: 1:80 (borderline). TH: 1:80. Single titre — antibodies may not yet be elevated at day 10. Non-diagnostic in isolation.
results.intestinal_phase: TO: 1:320 (positive). TH: 1:160 (positive). Rising titre strongly supports enteric fever.
results.haemodynamic_compromise: TO: 1:640 (very high). TH: 1:320. Established enteric fever confirmed serologically.

interpretation.early_febrile: Low single Widal titre does NOT exclude enteric fever — antibodies lag 7–10 days. Blood culture is more reliable at this stage. In Nepal, background titres of 1:80 are common from prior exposure.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Titre of 1:320 in a symptomatic patient in an endemic area is highly suggestive. Treat accordingly.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Very high titre confirms established disease. Note: antibiotic treatment may blunt further titre rise.

tests.name: Blood Culture
tests.fullName: Blood Culture and Sensitivity — Aerobic and Anaerobic x2 sets

results.early_febrile: POSITIVE at 72 hours: Salmonella typhi isolated. Sensitive to: Ceftriaxone, Azithromycin. RESISTANT to: Ampicillin, Chloramphenicol (MDR strain).
results.intestinal_phase: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi. Same sensitivity pattern. Note: sensitivity drops to ~50% in week 2.
results.haemodynamic_compromise: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi + E. coli isolated (polymicrobial — from intestinal source). Broad-spectrum cover required.

interpretation.early_febrile: Gold standard — positive in ~80% of first-week cases. Confirms diagnosis AND guides antibiotic choice. MDR strain present — chloramphenicol and ampicillin are inactive.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Still useful but reduced sensitivity. Confirms sensitivities for ongoing management.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Polymicrobial growth indicates intestinal perforation with faecal contamination.

results.early_febrile: Bilirubin: 1.8 mg/dL (mildly elevated). AST: 78 U/L (~2x ULN). ALT: 65 U/L (elevated). ALP: Normal. Pattern: mild hepatitis.
results.intestinal_phase: Bilirubin: 2.4 mg/dL. AST: 120 U/L. ALT: 98 U/L. Worsening transaminitis.
results.haemodynamic_compromise: Bilirubin: 4.2 mg/dL (clinical jaundice). PT prolonged. Coagulopathy developing.

interpretation.early_febrile: Mild transaminitis occurs in 20–40% of enteric fever cases. Helps distinguish from viral hepatitis (which shows higher ALT, conjugated bilirubin rise, and no leucopenia).
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Worsening hepatic involvement. Monitor for acute liver failure.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Coagulopathy with jaundice significantly increases surgical risk if perforation requires laparotomy.

tests.name: Dengue NS1 + IgM/IgG
tests.fullName: Dengue Serology — NS1 Antigen + IgM/IgG ELISA

results.early_febrile: NS1 Antigen: NEGATIVE. Dengue IgM: NEGATIVE. Dengue IgG: NEGATIVE. Dengue excluded.

interpretation.early_febrile: Dengue excluded — important differential for fever with thrombocytopenia in Nepal. NS1 negativity by day 10 would be expected even if dengue were the cause, so IgM negativity is the key exclusion here.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Dengue not contributing. Thrombocytopenia is from enteric fever, not dengue.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Dengue not the cause of deterioration.

results.early_febrile: Hepatomegaly (14.5 cm). Mild splenomegaly (12 cm). Multiple enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes at RIF. No free fluid. Gallbladder: normal.
results.intestinal_phase: Hepatosplenomegaly increased. Thickened bowel wall at terminal ileum. Small mesenteric nodes persisting. No free fluid yet.
results.haemodynamic_compromise: Free fluid in peritoneum (ascites). Dilated bowel loops. Discontinuity at terminal ileum region — possible perforation. Emergency CT or direct surgical exploration indicated.

interpretation.early_febrile: Classic sonographic triad: hepatomegaly + splenomegaly + mesenteric lymphadenopathy — highly characteristic of enteric fever. Helps exclude biliary disease and appendicitis.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Terminal ileal wall thickening reflects Peyer's patch involvement — characteristic of enteric fever. No free fluid yet.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Free intraperitoneal fluid strongly suggests intestinal perforation. Surgical emergency.

results.early_febrile: Processing (72 hours). Low positivity in week 1. No Salmonella seen on direct smear.
results.intestinal_phase: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi isolated in stool. Sensitive to Ceftriaxone and Azithromycin.
results.haemodynamic_compromise: POSITIVE: Salmonella typhi + E. coli — mixed bowel flora, consistent with intestinal perforation.

interpretation.early_febrile: Blood culture is more useful in week 1. Salmonella shedding in stool peaks in weeks 2–3.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Positive stool culture in week 2 confirms diagnosis. Useful also for contact tracing.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Mixed flora confirms intestinal source of contamination.

results.early_febrile: Mildly cloudy. WBC: Trace. Protein: Trace (non-specific). Glucose: Absent. No bacteria on microscopy.
results.intestinal_phase: Trace WBCs. No bacteria. Urine culture (if sent): Salmonella typhi isolated (shed in urine in ~25% of cases).
results.haemodynamic_compromise: Oliguria — urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr. Concentrated urine. Reflects pre-renal AKI from septic shock.

interpretation.early_febrile: UTI excluded. Trace abnormalities are non-specific. Not helpful for diagnosis.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Salmonella in urine is a secondary diagnostic clue — not the primary route. Does not change management.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Oliguria signals inadequate renal perfusion. Aggressive IV resuscitation required.

results.early_febrile: Clear lung fields. No consolidation, no pleural effusion. Heart size normal. Unremarkable.
results.intestinal_phase: Mild basal atelectasis bilaterally — non-specific, from abdominal distension. No pneumonia.
results.haemodynamic_compromise: Free gas under right dome of diaphragm — PNEUMOPERITONEUM. Confirms intestinal perforation. Surgical emergency.

interpretation.early_febrile: Normal CXR. Pneumonia and TB excluded as primary diagnosis at this stage. Not useful for diagnosing enteric fever.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Basal atelectasis from abdominal distension — non-specific. No change in management.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Pneumoperitoneum on CXR confirms perforation. Emergency laparotomy is mandatory.

results.early_febrile: Na: 134 mEq/L (mildly low). K: 3.4 mEq/L (low-normal). Cl: 98 mEq/L. HCO3: 22 mEq/L. Mild hyponatraemia consistent with prolonged fever and poor intake.
results.intestinal_phase: Na: 130 mEq/L (worsening hyponatraemia). K: 3.0 mEq/L (hypokalaemia from diarrhoea). HCO3: 20 mEq/L.
results.haemodynamic_compromise: Na: 126 mEq/L. K: 2.8 mEq/L (severe). HCO3: 14 mEq/L (metabolic acidosis from septic shock). Lactate: 5.2 mmol/L.

interpretation.early_febrile: Mild hyponatraemia from SIADH — a recognised feature of enteric fever. Guides IV fluid replacement (use NS, not hypotonic fluids).
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Hypokalaemia from diarrhoeal losses — requires active replacement. Monitor for arrhythmia.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Severe metabolic acidosis with elevated lactate — septic shock. Aggressive resuscitation and surgical source control required.

tests.name: Scrub Typhus IFA
tests.fullName: Scrub Typhus Indirect Immunofluorescence Assay (IFA) — Orientia tsutsugamushi

results.early_febrile: Scrub Typhus IFA: NEGATIVE (titre <1:80). No evidence of Orientia tsutsugamushi infection.
results.intestinal_phase: Scrub Typhus IFA: NEGATIVE. Repeat titre: Negative.

interpretation.early_febrile: Scrub typhus excluded — important differential in Nepal for prolonged fever. Scrub typhus classically has an eschar, which is absent here. No rash in hair-bearing areas. IFA negative.
interpretation.intestinal_phase: Scrub typhus not contributing to presentation.
interpretation.haemodynamic_compromise: Not the cause of haemodynamic compromise.

diagnosisOptions.label: Malaria — P. falciparum or P. vivax

general.name: IV Fluids
general.fullName: IV Normal Saline 0.9% or Ringer's Lactate 1L 8-hourly

early_febrile.note: Oral rehydration preferred if tolerating. IV for persistent fever and poor intake. Normal saline preferred given mild hyponatraemia.

intestinal_phase.note: IV fluids important — diarrhoea and poor oral intake causing dehydration and hypokalaemia. Add KCl supplementation.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Aggressive IV resuscitation for septic shock — 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus per Surviving Sepsis Campaign. Monitor urine output.

early_febrile.note: Correct antipyretic — symptomatic relief. NSAIDs must be avoided in enteric fever due to GI bleeding risk.

intestinal_phase.note: Continue paracetamol only. NSAIDs absolutely contraindicated in intestinal phase.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Paracetamol for fever. IV route if oral not tolerated. NSAIDs remain contraindicated.

early_febrile.note: NSAIDs increase risk of GI bleeding over the inflamed Peyer's patches in enteric fever. Paracetamol is the correct choice.

intestinal_phase.note: DANGEROUS — NSAIDs contraindicated. High risk of intestinal haemorrhage from ulcerated Peyer's patches.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: CRITICAL ERROR — NSAIDs in complicated disease causes catastrophic intestinal bleeding.

general.name: Monitoring
general.fullName: Vitals + Stool Output + Hepatic Monitoring + Daily Abdominal Exam

early_febrile.note: Monitoring initiated. Watch for sudden abdominal pain, melaena, or change in pulse.

intestinal_phase.note: Close monitoring for perforation signs — sudden severe pain, peritonism, melaena, or new tachycardia.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: ICU-level monitoring. Hourly urine output, serial abdominal examination, continuous cardiac monitoring.

typhoid.name: IV Ceftriaxone
typhoid.fullName: Inj. Ceftriaxone 2 g IV OD x 10–14 days (WHO first-line for enteric fever — Harrison's)

early_febrile.note: First-line treatment. Fever begins settling in 3–5 days. MDR sensitivity confirmed. Full course required.

intestinal_phase.note: Effective even in week 2. Patient will recover with close monitoring for intestinal complications.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Antibiotics alone insufficient in intestinal perforation. Surgical source control is also required.

typhoid.name: Oral Azithromycin
typhoid.fullName: Tab. Azithromycin 1 g OD x 5 days (WHO guideline — uncomplicated enteric fever)

early_febrile.note: Good oral option for uncomplicated enteric fever. Effective against MDR strains. Patient recovers.

intestinal_phase.note: Suboptimal in established disease. IV Ceftriaxone preferred at this stage. Partial benefit only.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Insufficient for complicated disease — IV antibiotics and surgery required. Oral route also unsafe.

typhoid.name: Chloramphenicol
typhoid.fullName: Tab. Chloramphenicol 500 mg QID x 14 days

early_febrile.note: Outdated. This isolate is MDR — culture confirms resistance to chloramphenicol. Also causes bone marrow suppression, worsening existing anaemia.

intestinal_phase.note: Chloramphenicol-resistant strain confirmed. Ineffective and risks aplastic anaemia.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Wrong drug for MDR strain. Bone marrow suppression in an already thrombocytopenic patient.

typhoid.name: Ciprofloxacin
typhoid.fullName: Tab. Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD x 10 days

early_febrile.note: Fluoroquinolone resistance in S. typhi is widespread in South Asia, including Nepal. Not recommended empirically per WHO guidelines. Risk of treatment failure.

intestinal_phase.note: Fluoroquinolone-resistant strain — ineffective. Disease progressing.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Inadequate coverage. Patient deteriorating on wrong antibiotic.

typhoid.name: Artemether-Lumefantrine
typhoid.fullName: Tab. Artemether-Lumefantrine 80/480 mg BD x 3 days (ACT — antimalarial)

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — giving antimalarials to a patient without malaria. MP smear and RDT are both negative. Delays correct treatment while enteric fever progresses.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER — wrong treatment for wrong disease. Intestinal phase advancing.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: FATAL BLUNDER — treating wrong disease during haemodynamic compromise.

typhoid.name: Emergency Laparotomy
typhoid.fullName: Emergency Laparotomy — Intestinal Perforation Repair + Peritoneal Washout

early_febrile.note: Surgery not indicated in uncomplicated enteric fever — patient has no signs of perforation. Unnecessary laparotomy with significant morbidity.

intestinal_phase.note: Surgery not yet indicated unless perforation is confirmed. Continue antibiotics and monitor closely.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Correct! Emergency laparotomy for intestinal perforation. Perforation repaired, peritoneal washout performed, drain placed. IV antibiotics continued post-op. Patient recovering in ICU.

typhoid.name: IV Dexamethasone
typhoid.fullName: Inj. Dexamethasone 3 mg/kg IV loading then 1 mg/kg 6-hourly (for severe typhoid with toxaemia — Davidson's, limited role)

early_febrile.note: Steroids not indicated in uncomplicated enteric fever. Reserved only for severe toxaemia with altered consciousness — not applicable here.

intestinal_phase.note: Steroids contraindicated in intestinal phase — increase risk of intestinal perforation and secondary infection.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Dexamethasone has a very limited role in severe enteric fever with shock per Davidson's — but is only adjunctive. Surgery and antibiotics take priority. Modest benefit if used correctly.

malaria.name: IV Artesunate
malaria.fullName: Inj. Artesunate 2.4 mg/kg IV at 0, 12, 24 hours (severe P. falciparum — WHO protocol)

early_febrile.note: MP smear and RDT both negative — treating wrong diagnosis. No benefit. Enteric fever untreated and progressing.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong drug for wrong disease. Expensive and ineffective.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Wrong treatment during haemodynamic compromise. Fatal delay.

malaria.name: Artemether-Lumefantrine
malaria.fullName: Tab. Artemether-Lumefantrine 80/480 mg BD x 3 days (P. falciparum ACT)

early_febrile.note: Malaria excluded on smear and RDT. ACT has no effect on Salmonella typhi.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong diagnosis. Enteric fever progressing.

malaria.name: Chloroquine
malaria.fullName: Tab. Chloroquine 10 mg base/kg Day 1 + 5 mg/kg Day 2–3 (P. vivax)

early_febrile.note: No malaria — chloroquine has no effect on enteric fever.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong diagnosis and wrong drug.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — enteric fever requires antibiotic treatment. Observation alone allows progression.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER — progression to perforation.

malaria.name: Primaquine
malaria.fullName: Tab. Primaquine 0.25 mg/kg OD x 14 days (radical cure — P. vivax hypnozoites)

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — primaquine risks haemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients. G6PD status unknown. No malaria confirmed. Dangerous and unnecessary.

intestinal_phase.note: DANGEROUS — drug-induced haemolytic anaemia worsens existing anaemia.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Haemolysis in a shocked patient is catastrophic.

dengue.name: Dengue Protocol
dengue.fullName: WHO Dengue Management — Haematocrit-Guided IV Fluid Resuscitation + Daily CBC Monitoring

early_febrile.note: Dengue NS1 and IgM are both negative. This is not dengue. Dengue protocol misses the required antibiotic treatment for enteric fever.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong diagnosis. Enteric fever advancing without antibiotics.

dengue.name: Platelet Transfusion
dengue.fullName: Single Donor Platelet Transfusion (SDP) — for dengue thrombocytopenia

early_febrile.note: Dengue excluded. Platelet transfusion for enteric fever thrombocytopenia is not indicated at this count (140,000/μL). Extremely expensive.

intestinal_phase.note: Platelets: 98,000/μL — not low enough to warrant transfusion (no active bleeding). Expensive and not indicated.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Platelet transfusion does not address the source of haemodynamic compromise (intestinal perforation). Surgery is required.

dengue.name: Antiviral Therapy
dengue.fullName: Experimental Antiviral (Ribavirin / Sofosbuvir — no approved dengue antiviral)

early_febrile.note: No approved antiviral for dengue exists. Dengue is also excluded on serology. Expensive, ineffective, and delays correct diagnosis.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong diagnosis and wrong drug.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — untreated enteric fever progresses.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER.

viral_hepatitis.name: Rest and Supportive Care
viral_hepatitis.fullName: Bed Rest + Low-fat Diet + ORS (Viral Hepatitis supportive management)

early_febrile.note: Hepatitis A/E management is supportive — but this is not viral hepatitis. No jaundice, ALP is normal, and bilirubin is only mildly elevated in an AST>ALT pattern. Enteric fever untreated.

intestinal_phase.note: Supportive care alone inadequate. Antibiotics not started.

viral_hepatitis.name: Hep A/E Serology
viral_hepatitis.fullName: Hepatitis A IgM + Hepatitis E IgM ELISA

early_febrile.note: No jaundice, no cholestatic picture, no elevated ALP. Hepatitis A/E is unlikely as the primary diagnosis. Low prior probability — expensive and unhelpful.

intestinal_phase.note: Not indicated. Enteric fever is the primary diagnosis.

viral_hepatitis.name: Ribavirin
viral_hepatitis.fullName: Ribavirin 400 mg BD x 14 days (chronic Hepatitis E — immunocompromised only)

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — Ribavirin is only for chronic Hep E in immunocompromised patients. Not indicated for acute hepatitis E, and this diagnosis is wrong entirely. Haemolytic anaemia risk.

intestinal_phase.note: Drug-induced haemolytic anaemia worsening existing anaemia.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER.

uti.name: Oral Nitrofurantoin
uti.fullName: Tab. Nitrofurantoin 100 mg BD x 5 days (uncomplicated UTI)

early_febrile.note: Urine is non-specifically abnormal — no WBC casts, no bacteria, no dysuria. UTI diagnosis is incorrect. Nitrofurantoin has no activity against Salmonella and does not treat systemic infection.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong diagnosis. Enteric fever advancing.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Inadequate for septic shock from enteric fever.

uti.name: Cotrimoxazole
uti.fullName: Tab. Cotrimoxazole 960 mg BD x 5 days (UTI treatment)

early_febrile.note: No evidence of UTI. Cotrimoxazole resistance is also high in S. typhi. Wrong diagnosis and insufficient even if partially applicable.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong drug, wrong disease.

early_febrile.note: Urine R/E shows only trace abnormalities with no bacteria. Prior probability of UTI is very low in this clinical picture. Urine culture has a 72-hour turnaround — will not guide acute management.

intestinal_phase.note: Not indicated. Enteric fever is the primary diagnosis.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Not relevant to the acute emergency.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER.

tb.name: Antitubercular Therapy
tb.fullName: HRZE Regimen — Isoniazid + Rifampicin + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol (standard ATT)

early_febrile.note: TB presents with months of symptoms, weight loss, night sweats, and cough. This patient has an acute 10-day illness with rose spots and relative bradycardia — not TB. Starting ATT unnecessarily has hepatotoxicity risk and delays correct treatment.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong diagnosis. Rifampicin hepatotoxicity can worsen existing transaminitis from enteric fever.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Drug-induced hepatotoxicity in a jaundiced septic patient.

tb.name: Mantoux Test
tb.fullName: Mantoux (Tuberculin Skin Test) — 5 TU PPD intradermal

early_febrile.note: Mantoux takes 48–72 hours to read and indicates only prior TB exposure — not active disease. Not useful in acute febrile illness management.

intestinal_phase.note: Slow test, not indicated in acute illness.

early_febrile.note: Dry non-productive cough is present but CXR is clear with no pulmonary infiltrates. TB sputum smear is low-yield in this presentation and does not address the acute febrile illness.

intestinal_phase.note: Not indicated. Enteric fever confirmed.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER.

leptospirosis.name: IV Benzylpenicillin
leptospirosis.fullName: Inj. Benzylpenicillin 1.5 MU QID IV x 7 days (leptospirosis treatment — Harrison's)

early_febrile.note: No conjunctival suffusion, no jaundice with renal failure, no calf tenderness — classic Weil's disease triad absent. Leptospirosis is unlikely. Penicillin has no effect on Salmonella.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong diagnosis. Enteric fever advancing.

leptospirosis.name: Doxycycline
leptospirosis.fullName: Tab. Doxycycline 100 mg BD x 7 days (mild leptospirosis)

early_febrile.note: Incorrect diagnosis. No leptospirosis features confirmed. Doxycycline has limited activity against Salmonella typhi.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong drug.

early_febrile.note: MAT is not indicated without classic leptospirosis features (jaundice + AKI + bleeding). Expensive, reference-lab only, slow. Dengue and enteric fever are more likely.

intestinal_phase.note: Not indicated. Expensive delay.

haemodynamic_compromise.note: Too late. Patient in critical condition.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER.

inf_mono.name: Acyclovir
inf_mono.fullName: Tab. Acyclovir 800 mg 5x/day x 7 days (EBV — not standard of care)

early_febrile.note: Acyclovir is not recommended for EBV per current guidelines. More importantly, this is not EBV — no tonsillar exudate, no posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, no atypical lymphocytes. Wrong diagnosis entirely.

intestinal_phase.note: Wrong drug, wrong disease.

inf_mono.name: Prednisolone (EBV)
inf_mono.fullName: Tab. Prednisolone 1 mg/kg OD (for severe EBV with thrombocytopenia)

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — steroids in enteric fever increase the risk of intestinal perforation. Also wrong diagnosis. Immunosuppression in a bacteraemic patient is dangerous.

intestinal_phase.note: DANGEROUS — steroids in the intestinal phase significantly increase perforation risk.

inf_mono.name: Ampicillin
inf_mono.fullName: Tab. Ampicillin 500 mg QID x 7 days

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — ampicillin causes a florid maculopapular rash in EBV (classic diagnostic trap). Also this is not EBV. Additionally, this S. typhi isolate is MDR and resistant to ampicillin — confirmed on blood culture.

intestinal_phase.note: Drug rash and ineffective treatment.

early_febrile.note: No tonsillar exudate, no posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, no splenomegaly consistent with EBV pattern. Low prior probability. Blood culture already growing Salmonella. Wasteful.

intestinal_phase.note: Not indicated. Diagnosis confirmed.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER.

brucellosis.name: Doxycycline + Rifampicin
brucellosis.fullName: Tab. Doxycycline 100 mg BD + Tab. Rifampicin 600 mg OD x 6 weeks (brucellosis — WHO/Harrison's)

early_febrile.note: Brucellosis requires livestock/animal contact or unpasteurised dairy — none reported. No joint pain, no undulant fever pattern. Blood culture has grown Salmonella, not Brucella. Wrong diagnosis and ineffective treatment.

intestinal_phase.note: Rifampicin hepatotoxicity worsens existing transaminitis from enteric fever.

early_febrile.note: No animal exposure history, no occupation risk. Brucella SAT is a low-priority test given the clinical picture and positive blood culture for Salmonella.

intestinal_phase.note: Not indicated.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER.

lymphoma.name: Empirical Steroids
lymphoma.fullName: Inj. Prednisolone 60 mg OD (empirical for lymphoma-related fever)

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — steroids in undiagnosed bacteraemia mask fever and signs, and dramatically increase intestinal perforation risk in enteric fever. No lymphoma features: no painless lymphadenopathy, no mediastinal mass, no night sweats pattern typical of lymphoma.

intestinal_phase.note: DANGEROUS — steroids in intestinal phase cause perforation.

lymphoma.name: Lymph Node Biopsy
lymphoma.fullName: Excision Biopsy of Lymph Node (for lymphoma diagnosis)

early_febrile.note: No peripheral lymphadenopathy present on examination. Lymph node biopsy cannot be performed. Acute onset 10-day illness is not consistent with lymphoma timeline. Expensive and misdirected.

intestinal_phase.note: Dangerous delay while enteric fever progresses to intestinal complications.

lymphoma.name: CT Neck/Chest/Abdomen
lymphoma.fullName: CT Neck + Chest + Abdomen + Pelvis with contrast (lymphoma staging)

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER — CT staging for lymphoma requires a tissue diagnosis first. No lymphadenopathy found on examination. Catastrophic budget overrun. Dangerous delay in starting antibiotics for bacteraemia.

intestinal_phase.note: Budget destroyed. Enteric fever advancing without treatment.

early_febrile.note: BLUNDER.

intestinal_phase.note: BLUNDER.

endMessages.perfect: Excellent! You identified enteric fever from the clinical triad, ordered targeted investigations including blood culture, and started first-line antibiotics promptly. Textbook management.
endMessages.good: Good clinical reasoning. Patient recovered. Minor suboptimal choices — perhaps unnecessary tests or slight treatment delay. Review Widal limitations and blood culture timing.
endMessages.average: Patient survived but management had errors. Delayed antibiotics, wrong empirical treatment, or excess investigations added avoidable risk. Review enteric fever management guidelines.
endMessages.poor: Patient developed serious complications. Enteric fever carries significant mortality only when missed or mismanaged — it is entirely treatable. Review this case carefully.
